reasoning: The ICE BofA US Corporate Effective Yield dropped sharply in late April 2025, falling from 5.41% on April 21 to 5.22% on April 25. This decisive downward momentum aligns with a broader decline in US Treasury yields as markets process cooler PCE inflation data, a potential economic slowdown, and the ripple effects of recent tariff/trade policy announcements. Bond strategists in a mid-April Reuters poll also predicted further fundamental declines in the 10-year Treasury yield toward 4.21% by June and 4.14% later in the year. Although widening corporate spreads during a trade slowdown could theoretically counteract falling Treasury yields, the dominant empirical force in recent days is unambiguously falling combined yields. Given the strong recent downward momentum, the probability of the index being higher than its late-April baseline is somewhat low in the short term (May 4, May 27). In accordance with forecasting principles, my confidence diminishes over longer horizons, meaning the probability estimates strictly pull closer to 0.5 for the more distant dates (July and October).
